segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with sweeping aerial views of major urban landscapes, beginning with San Francisco’s skyline under a hazy sky, featuring the Bay Bridge and a large cargo ship on the water, then transitioning to New York City, showcasing the Brooklyn Bridge and Manhattan’s dense skyline under clear blue skies, all set to an upbeat, slightly dramatic background music. A narrator's voice begins, stating, "The economy has been growing steadily for over a century, creating prosperity and security. But everything has its limits." The scene shifts to an aerial shot of a vast, organized agricultural field with rows of crops, followed by a desert landscape with a winding river and sparse vegetation. A man in a light purple shirt and glasses, seated in an office with a framed certificate and books, speaks about the need for "chips, energy, food," emphasizing that "we're going to need it for centuries and centuries and millennia, and for as long as humans are around." The narrator returns, explaining, "And all of these things need water." The video then cuts to a bustling city street with a time-lapse of pedestrians crossing a zebra crossing, followed by a crowded interior of Grand Central Terminal with people moving rapidly. A male voice with a German accent is heard saying, "Wasser in Hülle und Fülle." Another man, wearing glasses and a dark blazer, is shown speaking outdoors, stating in a British accent, "The days when we had abundant water supplies are gone too." The scene changes to a lively outdoor market with colorful stalls and people browsing, then shows a woman in a gray top and black pants bending over to fill a blue container from a public water spigot. The narrator's voice explains, "In many regions of the world, water has run out," followed by a female voice in Spanish saying, "Ni una gota," and another female voice translating, "No water at all, not a drop." An aerial view of a large industrial building with a red roof appears next, followed by a close-up of a man in a dark jacket and cap walking through a grassy field. The narrator declares, "The economy versus humanity versus nature." The video then shows a factory interior with rows of green bottles on a conveyor belt, accompanied by a subtle whirring sound. A man in a dark suit and tie speaks indoors in Spanish, saying, "Un derecho que tenemos es," which is translated by another voice as, "We have a right to clean water." The final scenes include a wide shot of a vast field of white crops under a cloudy sky, and a man in a checkered shirt sitting at a desk in a modern office. He speaks in German, which is translated by a British-accented voice as, "Globally, 70% of water goes to irrigation," and then another voice adds, "Look, you need water to make food."

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a close-up of the Man in Checkered Shirt, seated in a modern office with glasses perched on his head, speaking thoughtfully as he states, "Agriculture uses a hell of a lot of water." A dramatic, suspenseful background music begins and continues throughout the segment. The scene quickly transitions to an outdoor shot of the Facebook headquarters sign at 1 Hacker Way, surrounded by trees under a clear sky. A different male voice then declares, "Water will be the new gold," as the visuals shift to a dramatic nighttime shot of a fountain with multiple illuminated water jets against a dark building facade. The focus then moves to the Man in Light Purple Shirt, seated in a well-lit office with large windows, gesturing as he speaks, with a framed certificate and books visible behind him, explaining that "they are not matching the reality of the disappearing water with their desire for economic growth." The music intensifies as a narrator's voice begins, stating, "We've always known water to be plentiful, but that's changing because of the climate crisis." This narration accompanies a series of abstract and symbolic visuals: a water droplet creating ripples, a bottle pouring water into a glass, a close-up of a water droplet on a leaf, and a swirling hurricane from space, all emphasizing water's importance and scarcity. The visuals then starkly contrast with a dry, shriveled corn cob and a hand sprinkling soil onto dry ground, symbolizing drought and arid conditions. The narrator continues, "With water becoming scarce, this series asks, what happens if we have no more water?" These powerful images culminate in a title card reading 'DocFilm DW THIRST When the Wells Run Dry' and 'The Last Resources A Film by Daniel Harrich' over a rippling water background, accompanied by the ongoing dramatic music. The final scenes show an aerial view of Potsdam, highlighting its historic buildings and lush greenery, followed by a close-up of a brick building with a plaque and a blue dome, which the narrator identifies as the Potsdam Institute for Climate Impact Research, stating it "has been warning about the effect of the climate crisis on the water supply."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Prof. Anders Levermann from the Potsdam Institute for Climate Impact Research, seated on a high stool in a grassy field before a large, historic brick building. He gestures expressively as he speaks in German, with an English voice-over translating his analogy about a falling phone to explain that understanding physics means we don't need an experiment to know what will happen. He continues, explaining that increasing atmospheric CO2 will warm the planet, leading to more water vapor, drought, and intense precipitation, and that water availability is decreasing. As he speaks, the scene transitions to aerial footage of a dam releasing cascading water, an interior shot of a factory bottling water, and a close-up of a smokestack emitting thick, white plumes, all set to subtle, tense background music. The video then cuts to Fred Hattermann, also from the Potsdam Institute, seated on a stool in a grassy area with scientific monitoring equipment behind him. He speaks in German, with an English voice-over, stating that as it gets warmer, even more water will be needed for agriculture, drinking, and industry. The visuals continue with aerial views of large industrial complexes and an interior view of a car manufacturing plant, showing robotic arms welding car bodies. Hattermann further elaborates, referencing Tesla's need for a large amount of local water, emphasizing the need to balance this with nature as current actions will have lasting effects. The segment concludes with a close-up of a red robotic arm actively welding a white car frame, while a male narrator adds that for its Gigafactory in Brandenburg, outside of Berlin, Tesla seeks access to especially valuable groundwater reserves.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens at a construction site in August 2021, where a media event is underway. A man in a dark suit and scarf, holding a microphone, engages in a lively discussion with a woman in a yellow vest and white hard hat, seen from behind, while a man in a blue blazer also holds a microphone nearby and a cameraman records the scene. The man in the dark suit, speaking with an English accent, dismisses concerns about water scarcity, laughing and stating, "This region has so much water. Look around you," and later, "No, this is completely wrong. It's like water everywhere here," and "Does this seem like a desert to you?" The woman, speaking with a German accent, counters that the local water supply indicates no more water for further development, and later thanks him in German amidst laughter. The scene then transitions to aerial views of vast industrial complexes, including a large white-roofed building and a sprawling BASF facility, followed by a busy port with cranes and shipping containers, all while a male narrator with an English accent explains that "Tesla's application hasn't yet been approved," and that "German industry now seems to be waking up to the increasing scarcity of water too." The video concludes in a grassy field where Fred Hattermann, identified by on-screen text as from the Potsdam Institute for Climate Impact Research, is seated among scientific monitoring instruments. He speaks animatedly in German, with the narrator translating his points about the dry years of 2018-2020 showing companies the importance of water, leading to a rush for permits, and the need to be aware of the true water supply to avoid selling it off. Throughout, ambient industrial sounds and background chatter are present at the construction site, transitioning to more open-air sounds during the aerial and field segments.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with an aerial view of Munich, showcasing a dense urban landscape, before transitioning to an office setting where Prof. Martin Grambow, an older man with white hair wearing a traditional Bavarian-style gray jacket, is seen writing in a notebook and then speaking while gesturing, with a framed black-and-white photo of the Golden Gate Bridge behind him. A light, investigative background music begins as an English voiceover introduces him as the chairman of the Federal Working Group on Water, who wants authorities to use restraint when allocating water rights. Prof. Grambow speaks in German, his words translated by the voiceover, explaining that due to a global water shortage, major water companies are seeking their excellent quality water. The scene then cuts to an aerial view of Lüneburg, a town with red-roofed buildings, followed by an industrial facility: a large red brick Coca-Cola European Partners building with its prominent logo. Inside, a water bottling factory is shown with rows of clear plastic bottles moving along a conveyor belt, being filled and capped with green caps. Stefan Kunerth, a man with short gray hair and glasses, wearing a blue button-up shirt, is then seen seated on a stool in the factory, speaking directly to the camera with industrial machinery and a Krones machine visible in the background. His German speech is translated by the voiceover, revealing that they have approval to draw 350,000 cubic meters from the aquifer and plan to double this amount with a third well. The segment concludes with a wide aerial shot of a rural landscape under a hazy sky, as the voiceover highlights "Coca-Cola's quest for water," with the music becoming more prominent.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a construction site in Rettenstedt, just outside Lüneburg, where a large yellow drilling machine is actively drilling a well 190 meters deep into the earth, as indicated by the narrator. A worker in blue overalls and a white cap operates the equipment behind a metal fence, with the distinct mechanical whirring and grinding sound of the drilling machine accompanying the scene. This operation, the narrator explains, puts the mayor in a dilemma. The scene transitions to an indoor office where Steffen Gärtner from the Town of Gellersen, a man in a dark blazer and glasses, speaks animatedly in German, gesturing at a table in a formal meeting room. The narrator translates his concern: "The question is simply how do we sell it? Or rather, how do we communicate to citizens that the groundwater that is actually there to ensure their supply of public drinking water is partially being given to the beverage industry, and then we have to tell citizens what they get in return." The narrative then shifts to a public protest in a town square, where "resistance is stirring in the region of Lüneburg," with more than 1,000 people protesting against Coca-Cola, fearing their water is being sold off. A crowd gathers under umbrellas, holding signs like 'WER UNSER WASSER NICHT SCHÜTZT, WIRD NICHT GEWÄHLT!' (If you don't protect our water, you won't get elected!) and 'Das Wasser gehört unseren Kindern' (The water belongs to our children). A woman sits on the ground with a sign, while a man in a blue vest speaks into a microphone from a podium, addressing the crowd in front of historic buildings. The segment concludes with a woman's voice from the protest shouting, "Liebe Lüneburger!" (Dear Lüneburgers!), emphasizing the community's activism and environmental advocacy.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Dr. Marianne Temmesfeld, identified by on-screen text as 'Our Water Lüneburg,' seated in a bright, sunlit room with large windows, gesturing expressively as she introduces herself in English, stating, "My name is Marianne Temmesfeld, and I am also a member of Our Water Lüneburg." She then speaks passionately in German, with an English voiceover, expressing her desire to "stop this well" and "prevent Coca-Cola from taking any more," anticipating that this would lead to them saying, "okay, then we can't produce any more beer." An upbeat, slightly dramatic background music begins as the scene transitions to a rainy protest in a town square, where demonstrators, many masked and holding umbrellas, march with signs, including one reading 'WASSER' and a large banner declaring 'WER UNSER WASSER NICHT SCHÜTZT, WIRD NICHT GEWÄHLT!' (If you don't protect our water, you won't get elected!). A woman with short gray hair, wearing a face mask, speaks passionately into a microphone to the crowd, amidst the audible sounds of rain and crowd murmurs. A male narrator confirms Dr. Marianne Temmesfeld's involvement in the citizens' initiative since early 2020. The video then shows a white building with a red-tiled roof, before moving inside to Jens Böther, identified as from Lüneburg. He is seen in a formal room, wearing a gray suit and striped tie, speaking thoughtfully, as the narrator explains that he "issues water extraction permits, even Coca-Cola's," and that "his decision will last for decades." Böther then speaks in German, with an English voiceover, explaining that "the groundwater authorities in Lüneburg first determine whether extraction is possible, and then we make a decision," acknowledging that "that can also mean that we have to make a decision that the town doesn't like." The scene transitions to the exterior and interior of the Lower Saxony Ministry for the Environment, Energy, Construction and Climate Protection. Inside, Olaf Lies, identified as from the Lower Saxony Environment Ministry, is interviewed in a modern office with nature-themed artwork, wearing a dark blue suit and patterned tie, speaking with a serious demeanor. The narrator clarifies that "water rights are up to local administrators," but "the state of Lower Saxony, where Lüneburg is located, is also dealing with this issue." Olaf Lies then speaks in German, with an English voiceover, stating that "Lower Saxony is quite diverse."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Dr. Marianne Temmesfeld, identified on-screen as 'Our Water Lüneburg,' seated at a white table in a sunlit room, speaking with a serious expression about how people are forced to buy water at high prices in supermarkets, using Mexico as an example. The scene transitions to an aerial view of an industrial complex labeled 'San Cristóbal,' as a male narrator, accompanied by background music, identifies it as a large Coca-Cola plant in Southern Mexico. A red Coca-Cola delivery truck is then seen driving through a gated area with barbed wire and chain-link fencing, with the narrator emphasizing the plant's protection. The truck continues along a road, passing a horse and rider, while the narrator states that Mexican health authorities report more of the beverage is consumed here than anywhere else in Mexico, averaging two liters per person per day. He adds that 'as long as Coca-Cola has enough water, business is good,' but for the people of San Cristóbal, 'life is very different,' as 'they are left high and dry.' The scene shifts to a group walking on a dirt path in a mountainous area, then focuses on Margarita Pale, identified on-screen, who bends to fill a container from a public water spigot. With a concerned expression, she speaks directly to the camera, lamenting in Spanish (translated by a female voice) that 'here is the faucet, and there is no water at all. Not a drop. Absolutely no water.' She continues, explaining that 'It's like this every day. The whole city, everywhere in the city. People are complaining in every neighborhood because they have no water.' The segment concludes with an aerial shot of a town nestled in a valley, bordered by mountains and a river with a highway, as the background music continues.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a man in a red cap and light-colored shirt walking toward a metal gate, unlocking it and entering a narrow alleyway. Inside, a woman in a gray top and purple apron is seen hanging laundry on a line, setting a scene of daily domestic activity, accompanied by gentle, melancholic background music. The scene shifts to Eustacio Juán Hernandez Vazquez, identified by on-screen text, a man with a red cap and brown jacket, moving a large terracotta container and speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures. A male narrator explains that Eustacio, a gardener, has no water, meaning no work. Eustacio then speaks in Spanish, stating, "Even now, we no longer have water," with the narrator translating and elaborating that they won't have water until Monday and must make do with what they have for food, noting the dry season is just beginning. The video then highlights a hand-painted sign reading 'CUIDA EL AGUA HOY TIENE UN GRAN VALOR PARA MAÑANA' (Take care of water, today it has great value for tomorrow) in front of a colorful mural, its message reinforced by the narrator. Finally, Margarita Pale, identified by on-screen text, appears, wearing a gray turtleneck and a light knitted scarf, speaking earnestly to the camera in front of a doorway. She begins in Spanish, mentioning, "Coca-Cola yes, they do have, they take water..." The narrator translates and expands, detailing how Coca-Cola extracts water 24 hours a day for soft drinks and bottled water sold across cities, countries, and states, while the people of San Cristobal lack access to their own water, as the audio fades.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a bustling street scene where masked pedestrians stroll past outdoor cafes under 'Heineken' and 'DIA FOLIS' umbrellas, while a woman in a beige shawl walks purposefully. A voice-over immediately highlights a critical issue, stating that people "have to buy water in plastic bottles." The camera then focuses on a red Coca-Cola delivery truck and a refrigerated display case stocked with various beverages, including 'Ciel' water. The voice-over explains that 'Ciel', despite its name meaning 'sky', is a Coca-Cola product "bottled from the ground." The scene shifts to Edgar Sulca Baéz from CESMECA University, who, speaking earnestly with expressive hand gestures, explains through a voice-over translation that the water authority, Conagua, granted Coca-Cola extensive licenses due to a former president, Vicente Fox, who worked for the company. An aerial view of the large 'San Cristóbal' industrial complex is shown as the voice-over describes Coca-Cola's "exclusive rights to the high-quality water of a once water-rich region," calling it an "extremely lucrative business." The red Coca-Cola truck is then seen driving through a gated, barbed-wire area, contrasting with a passing horse and rider. Edgar Sulca Baéz concludes, revealing that Coca-Cola "pumps more than 1.2 million liters of water every day" from three wells to produce and sell their products from San Cristóbal, while "the surrounding neighborhoods don't have enough" water.

The narrative returns to the ground with a red Coca-Cola delivery truck, its side open to reveal stacked crates, driving through a gated industrial area, then parked near a building with barbed wire fencing, accompanied by the sound of the truck driving. A prominent blue water tower stands against a partly cloudy sky, symbolizing local water infrastructure, as the narrator explains that Coca-Cola, when asked for comment, stated its awareness of the water problem and helps local residents install water tanks, rainwater, and other collection devices. segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of the Man in Light Purple Shirt, a middle-aged man with gray hair and glasses, seated in a car and speaking conversationally while driving past industrial containers and construction equipment under a bright, hazy sky. He describes the immense "scope of this place" that "stretches and stretches," and notes that a "water facility is about the size of a facility that you'd see for a small city." The scene transitions to a high-tech manufacturing environment, showcasing a yellow robotic machine precisely handling a circular silicon wafer, with digital readouts displaying negative values like '-0.93' and '-0.80', indicating a controlled, automated process. An exterior shot of a modern Intel building with a large glass facade bearing the 'Intel' logo and the slogan 'Do something wonderful' is then shown. A narrator's voice questions, "Microchip production requires a huge amount of ultra-pure water. But Intel has been expanding in Phoenix for more than 25 years anyway. Why invest in the middle of the desert?" The camera then cuts to Todd Brady, identified by on-screen text as an Intel representative, seated on a stool in front of a large window overlooking a sprawling industrial complex. He wears a dark blazer over a light shirt and speaks articulately and expressively, using hand gestures to emphasize his points. He acknowledges, "We are here in the middle of the desert. It is warm, there's no question about it," and explains that Intel considers "many factors into consideration when we decide where to build," including a "favorable business environment," a "strong workforce," and crucial "infrastructure including utilities, water," which is "very critical to us as well," noting they evaluated Arizona "25 plus years ago." The background reveals a sprawling industrial site with storage tanks, parking lots, and distant structures under a clear sky, consistent with daytime, and the overall atmosphere is professional and forward-looking, focusing on innovation and technology.

The camera follows the two men as they walk across a dry, tilled field, with Robert Schettler, wearing sunglasses and an IID logo polo, speaking directly to the camera. He explains that "IID's main concern is the protection of its water rights," which have been "argued over the years," but are crucial for "agricultural purposes," acknowledging "municipalities and industry" also use water. Schettler stresses the importance of retaining these rights "because we are providing food and fiber for the nation." The video continues with sweeping aerial views of large, tilled fields, some being irrigated, and a tall power transmission tower, emphasizing the scale of agricultural operations in the bright, sunny Imperial Valley. The narrator concludes by reiterating, "Without water, there is no agriculture," and questions "how effectively is agriculture using the precious resource?"

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of Robert Schettler, a man with gray hair and a beard, wearing a dark blue Imperial Irrigation District (IID) polo shirt and sunglasses, speaking in a rural agricultural setting under a clear blue sky. He states that areas like the Valley in LA are booming with people and need more water, leading them to "come looking for us," describing it as an "ongoing battle" where they "hold our ground" because they "believe in what we do." Light, dramatic background music begins as the scene transitions to sweeping aerial views of vast, dry agricultural fields with irrigation pipes laid out in parallel rows, some actively spraying water, highlighting the contrast between irrigated and arid land. A narrator explains that the Imperial Irrigation District fields use about 3.7 trillion gallons to water crops each year, an "unimaginably large amount" now lacking elsewhere on the country's West Coast. The camera then shifts to a wide shot of a dry, cracked earth field with irrigation pipes, emphasizing water scarcity. The scene changes to a panoramic view of San Francisco, with the city skyline partially shrouded in fog and the Bay Bridge visible, labeled 'San Francisco' on screen. The narrator identifies San Francisco as the "gateway to Silicon Valley," home to the world's largest tech companies and a center for innovation. The video then cuts to a street view showing a Netflix sign and a large billboard with the Facebook 'like' thumbs-up logo and '1 Hacker Way' text, followed by an aerial shot of the iconic circular Apple Park building, all representing the tech hub. The narrative then moves to a drought-stricken reservoir, identified by the narrator as Anderson Reservoir, which is "nearly empty at only 3% of its capacity," posing a "serious threat." The water is even running out "in the country's richest city." Receding water levels reveal dry, cracked earth and exposed banks. Two men in yellow safety vests, one pointing toward the reservoir, are shown discussing the situation, with one wearing a light purple shirt and the other a white shirt, both with sunglasses, implied to be "managers from Valley Water." The final shot is a close-up of the reservoir’s edge, showing the stark contrast between the remaining water and the dry, rocky shoreline under a bright sun, as the somber music continues.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with Gary Kremen from the Silicon Valley Water Board, standing outdoors in a dry, arid landscape under a clear blue sky, with rolling golden hills and a dried-up reservoir emphasizing drought conditions. Wearing a bright yellow safety vest over a light purple shirt, he gestures expressively as he speaks with a clear, urgent tone about the rising price of "import water," which is "five times what it was two years ago," and the limited ability of people to pay. He stresses that "we also can't get it here even if we can buy it," because "there's no water to import anymore." Dramatic, slightly ominous background music then begins as a male narrator declares that "The water crisis has unleashed a modern gold rush." The scene transitions to an indoor office where financial investor John Vidovich, in a blue and white checkered shirt, is seen interacting at a reception desk before being seated at a large wooden conference table. The narrator explains that "Businessmen like real estate magnate John Vidovich are investing in water," and that "American water law suits them just fine," as "Whoever owns the land also owns the water rights." With glasses perched on his head, Vidovich, identified by on-screen text, thoughtfully answers an off-screen interviewer's question about his land ownership, stating in a calm, measured voice that he owns "somewhere between 100 and 200,000 acres of agricultural and grazing land." When asked about water ownership, he clarifies, "Nobody, I don't think anyone owns the water. You have a right to use it." The DW logo remains visible throughout.

During this narration, the video cuts back to Cannon Michael in a closer shot with a red barn and trees in the background, then shifts to a vintage black-and-white photograph of a large, ornate Victorian-style house. segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with sweeping aerial shots of a large-scale industrial dairy farm, showcasing rows of black and white cattle in long, narrow pens with concrete feed alleys and dark, manure-filled areas, as a narrator states that "water goes to agriculture" and "we feed more than half of our grain to animals." The camera glides over the expansive, arid landscape, revealing a large, white, foamy manure lagoon, while the narrator emphasizes that "no food requires as much water as meat." A broader aerial view of the farm complex, including barns, storage units, and a network of roads, highlights the scale of the operation, as the narrator poses the question, "What if this agriculture and cattle region runs out of water?" and recounts how "just how quickly the water would disappear here was also underestimated" in Saskatoon, Canada. The scene transitions to a group of black cattle in a fenced enclosure, then cuts to a close-up of Scott Wright from the University of Saskatchewan conversing with a man in a light gray polo shirt, who, with a clear, slightly gravelly voice, explains that "we need as human beings water, a good water supply. Cattle are no different," and that "they need a large volume of water because they have a very big stomach on them." Aerial views return, showing a circular, shallow pond with white sediment and a few birds, followed by more black cattle in pens, as the narrator somberly notes that "people lack water, animals lack water," and "farmers have so little water, they can't even grow enough feed." The segment concludes with a close-up of the man in the light gray polo shirt, speaking earnestly with the sun flaring behind him, describing the situation as "very devastating" and the "tough decision" for farmers who "don't have the feed to overwinter their cattle so they're going to have to put them through the sale system."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens in a dry, open rural setting under a bright, slightly overcast sky, where a man with gray hair, glasses, and a beard, wearing a light gray polo shirt, speaks expressively to another man in a blue patterned shirt. The man in the light gray polo discusses the devastating impact of herd reduction, explaining that farmers are forced to sell off 10-30% of their cattle, sometimes reducing herds from 400 to 100-200 head, a process that takes generations to build and years to rebuild. Somber orchestral music begins, and a narrator's voice highlights that "psychological pressure is affecting the cattle farmers as much as economic hardships." The scene then cuts to a weathered white livestock trailer, containing black cattle, parked near metal pens, with the sounds of cattle mooing and general farm ambiance audible. The camera pans along the trailer before returning to the two men, who continue their serious conversation near the pens with cattle visible in the background. The man in the blue patterned shirt asks if "suicide is an issue here," to which the man in the light gray polo confirms it is, mentioning that government support programs are only one-year, "ad hoc" initiatives designed to help farmers "stick handle through this drought situation."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a stark aerial view of a vast, dry, cracked reservoir or salt flat under a clear blue sky, with a small vehicle driving along an adjacent road, visually emphasizing water scarcity, accompanied by gentle, contemplative background music. The scene transitions to an aerial view of Saskatoon, showcasing autumn-colored trees and modern buildings, including a large institutional complex with a satellite dish. A male narrator identifies this as the location of the Global Institute for Water Security at the state-run University of Saskatchewan, where J Famiglietti analyzes satellite data. The camera then focuses on a brick sign reading '11 Innovation Boulevard' with a moose sculpture, before a man in a beige jacket and backpack enters a building through glass doors. Inside, J Famiglietti, a man in a light gray polo shirt with glasses and a beard, stands before a large screen displaying a satellite map of North America. He gestures as he explains the color-coded anomalies from 2006 to 2012, noting that "India was one of the first places where we did in-depth analysis... because we could see this tremendous hotspot from space," attributing the dramatic red hotspot over the western United States to "the depletion of groundwater that was driving this tremendous water loss." The video then cuts to an aerial view of Punjab, India, with lush green fields and waterlogged paddies. The narrator explains that "Northern India lives from the water that comes from the rivers of the Himalayas," but "the climate crisis is changing the water supply at an unimaginable speed." A close-up shows a farmer in a pink sweater and white turban inspecting a crop of mustard or canola, as the narrator adds that "water lost here is almost the highest in the world," yet "because the soil in the region is particularly fertile, Punjab's agriculture can still feed up to 1 billion..."

General Saigal continues, stating Pakistan claims India's use of western river water is stopping the complete flow to eastern rivers, interfering with crop growth, causing joblessness, and spreading famine ('bhukhmari phail rahi hai') in Pakistan, leading to increased terrorist acts. The scene shifts to armed soldiers in camouflage gear patrolling a rainy urban street near a white car and motorcycle, with a 'KING SPORTS' sign visible. The video then shifts to a middle-aged man in a light gray polo shirt and glasses, who stands before a large screen displaying a satellite map of South Asia. He gestures towards the map, speaking in English about the implications of groundwater changes, as the 'Equivalent Height Anomaly' data progresses from 2005 to 2013, highlighting a growing red anomaly over northern India. He explains the potential impact on the 1.5 to 2 billion people in the region if Himalayan snowpack disappears and glaciers melt, leading to unreplenished rivers and aquifers, raising concerns about conflict, climate refugees, livelihoods, and political stability. The map zooms out to a broader view, revealing the 'NASA GRACE Mission' text, as a narrator emphasizes that "NASA data has provided early warnings about water availability." The scene cuts to a close-up of the same man, now in front of a different map showing groundwater changes in Germany, where he continues to discuss regional water loss, with the narrator asking about the situation in Germany.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a scientist in a light gray polo shirt, speaking with a male voice about how the Lüneburg region has been "really impacted quite significantly," as he gestures towards a large screen displaying a NASA map showing water storage depletion across Europe. A narrator then highlights that the trend for the Lüneburg region is clear, with the water supply deteriorating significantly over the last 20 years, while a line graph illustrates this downward trend in water equivalent height. The scene transitions to an aerial view of Lüneburg, followed by an industrial building with the 'KRONES' logo, as the narrator reveals that Coca-Cola, Germany's largest beverage producer, plans to drill a third well and double its capacity. This prompts a protest in a town square, where demonstrators hold signs like 'WIR BRAUCHEN WASSER' and a large banner reading 'WEDER WASSER NOCH FÖRDERUNG', with the narrator explaining that a citizens' initiative is protesting and politician Jens Böther is weighing the company's application. Jens Böther, seated in a modern office, is then shown as the narrator mentions he doesn't yet know the results of the NASA mission. An interviewer's voice (translated from German) asks Böther if he would have to step in if the situation worsens. Böther, speaking thoughtfully with a German accent, responds that their mandate is to protect groundwater and if the long-term supply is at risk, they would have to implement measures like using water sparingly, reusing it for irrigation, or finding technological solutions.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens at a construction site where a man in a dark suit and scarf, holding a microphone, sarcastically asks reporters, "Does this seem like a desert to you?" amidst laughter, while a man in a blue blazer stands nearby. Another voice interjects with "Okay, danke schön," before the first speaker adds, "That's ridiculous." Somber, atmospheric music begins as the scene cuts to a wide aerial shot of a vast, receding reservoir, revealing exposed dry land and numerous boats stranded on the shore. A calm male narrator's voice overlays this, stating, "Musk should be more acutely aware of the dangers of water scarcity than anyone," adding that "Millions of people in his home state on the West Coast of the US, where his company is headquartered, are already feeling the threat." The camera then focuses on Jay Famiglietti, identified by on-screen text, walking along the sandy shore of the same receding reservoir before sitting on a rock under a tree. Speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures, he asserts that what was once considered "all right" no longer exists, emphasizing, "everything is not going to be all right." He explains that "the only choice is to adapt to the water that we have available," warning that without adaptation, "then it's like the apocalypse." The segment concludes with further aerial views of the reservoir’s expansive, dry shoreline, as credits roll over a shot of a smokestack emitting thick black smoke, with text identifying the film as 'Shot in Mexico' and 'A Film by DANIEL HARRICH,' while the dramatic music intensifies.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with a scene at a construction site where a man in a dark suit and scarf (Man in Dark Suit and Scarf) and a man in a blue blazer (Man in Blue Blazer) are being interviewed by reporters wearing hard hats and high-visibility vests. The setting is outdoors, with a large concrete structure in the background, and microphones are pointed at the two men as they speak. The camera then cuts to a wide aerial shot of a vast, receding reservoir, revealing exposed dry land and numerous boats stranded on the shore, indicating severe water depletion. The scene transitions to a man in a light green polo shirt and jeans (Jay Famiglietti) walking along a sandy shore toward the water, then sitting on a rock under a tree, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures. The background features the same reservoir with boats and distant mountains. The video concludes with aerial views of the reservoir’s expansive, dry shoreline, followed by credits rolling over a shot of a smokestack emitting black smoke, with text identifying the film’s crew and location: 'Shot in Mexico' and 'A Film by DANIEL HARRICH'.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a wide, static shot of a vast, arid, and barren landscape under a clear blue sky, depicting a desert or drought-affected region with sparse, dry shrubs dotting the pale, cracked earth. A low, sustained, and slightly metallic drone sound begins, setting an eerie and desolate tone. In the distance, faint industrial structures and power lines are visible, hinting at human presence. As the camera slowly pans right, the terrain remains desolate, with tire tracks cutting through the dry ground, emphasizing human activity in an otherwise lifeless environment. The drone sound deepens, becoming more resonant and mournful, intensifying the sense of urgency. The scene gradually transitions to reveal a body of water in the distance, possibly a receding lake or reservoir, with the horizon line becoming more defined. The camera continues its slow pan, and the water becomes more prominent, appearing shallow and still. As the pan concludes, a large, rusted red sign with bold white letters spelling 'S.O.S.' emerges from the shallow water, partially submerged and anchored by metal supports, standing as a stark symbol of distress and environmental crisis. Throughout the sequence, white text overlays appear on the right side of the screen, crediting the production team, including camera operators Walter Harrich and Ray Marmolejo, video editors Michael Fungone and Constantin Dauch, shoot in Mexico by Wolf-Dieter Vogel, production manager Thomas Lorenz, commissioning editor Thomas Reutter, producer Hans-Michael Kassel, executive producer Danuta Harrich-Zandberg, and production by DIWAFILM GMBH for SWR, with an English version by Deutsche Welle in 2022. The overall atmosphere is one of profound desolation, urgency, and environmental warning, with the 'S.O.S.' sign serving as the focal point of human desperation in the face of ecological collapse, all underscored by the pervasive, unsettling drone.
